Good morning,

This Daily Update interview is another installment of the Stratechery Found=
er series; as a reminder, one of the challenges in covering startups is the=
 lack of available data. My solution is to go in the opposite direction and=
 interview founders directly, letting them give their subjective overview o=
f their companies, while pressing them on their business model, background,=
 and long-term potential.

Today=E2=80=99s interview is with Guillermo Rauch ( https://twitter.com/rau=
chg ) , founder and CEO of Vercel, along with the open source Next.js frame=
work for React. Next.js allows front-end developers to built React-based we=
b applications with server side rendering that are dynamically tailored to =
individual customers; those applications can be easily deployed using Verce=
l, without any need to pre-configure or provision cloud instances. Vercel=
=E2=80=99s last raise of $150 million was in November 2021, at a valuation =
of over $2.5 billion.

In this interview Rauch traces not only his history, but also that of React=
 and Next.js, and explains why Vercel and Next.js have always been inextric=
ably tied together. We discuss whether or not it is a business challenge bu=
ilding on open source, and why Vercel can re-sell cloud services with a hea=
lthy margin without its customers fleeing. We also touch on Vercel=E2=80=99=
s go-to-market, fundraising in 2021, and Rauch=E2=80=99s ambitious vision f=
or the future.

To listen to this interview as a podcast, click the link at the top of this=
 email to add Stratechery to your podcast player.

sales=E2=80=9D. A couple of years ago, a more striking result was published=
 by Deloitte and Google ( https://www2.deloitte.com/content/dam/Deloitte/ie=
/Documents/Consulting/Milliseconds_Make_Millions_report.pdf ) , I don=E2=80=
=99t know if you=E2=80=99ve heard about this one. On mobile, mobile web =E2=
=80=94 an even more challenging of an environment than desktop =E2=80=94 wh=
ich is what Amazon published those results, a hundred milliseconds improvem=
ent in load times across travel, luxury and retail yielded an 8% lift in co=
nversion.

This is actually pretty straightforward. If you=E2=80=99re on Instagram and=
 you tap on a product that you like, on an ad, or you=E2=80=99re on Twitter=
, or you get a text message with a link, if that thing doesn=E2=80=99t load=
 instantly, your attention goes elsewhere. So when you=E2=80=99re thinking =
about a commercial transaction, the math of improving the frontend speed is=
 so easy, because I make things faster, I convert better. So if you=E2=80=
=99re in one of those specific verticals that I just mentioned =E2=80=94 tr=
avel, luxury retail, marketing =E2=80=94 the ROI on the infrastructure side=
 is just pretty much self-explanatory.

*Right, this gets the being a profit center versus the cost center and you =
can certainly see a dynamic in companies where developers are on your side,=
 there=E2=80=99s an SVP-level or COO-level that=E2=80=99s a little iffy abo=
ut the cost, but you can bring the CEO in on top and say, =E2=80=9CLook, we=
=E2=80=99re driving top line=E2=80=9D. It=E2=80=99s a simplified idea here,=
 but have you had to construct a sales team to actually execute that or is =
it still happening organically? Is that something down the road that you=E2=
=80=99re going need to deal with? This does get into what is the next steps=
 for Vercel. Is it just developing the product or do you need to refine tha=
t motion to get broader buy-in?*

*GR:* Yeah, of course as the deployments of Vercel get more ambitious and a=
s our platform replaces larger and larger workloads, and the contract lengt=
hs get longer and we do more multi-year deals, the conversation becomes a l=
ot more about strategic alignment. It=E2=80=99s not just like, =E2=80=9COh,=
 the developer likes Next.js=E2=80=9D, of course. So our sales team has tho=
se conversations with our strategic target accounts on a regular basis.

*With that future point, you raised $150 million in 2021 ( https://vercel.c=
om/blog/vercel-funding-series-d-and-valuation ) at a valuation of over $2.5=
 billion. That was a very different fundraising environment, I think it=E2=
=80=99s safe to say, do you have any regrets about that or any challenges t=
hat you foresee? What=E2=80=99s the landscape for you as an operating entit=
y?*

*GR:* At the time, of course we were in a different environment, but I reme=
mber rooting myself in a simple fact, which is Vercel=E2=80=99s ambition is=
 to run the entire Internet, we want to power every single instance of the =
web. Anytime you=E2=80=99re deploying a experience for a user, it=E2=80=99s=
 a Vercel URL. So when you have that ambition in mind and that total addres=
sable market, sure maybe at that given time the fundraising environment was=
 different, but this journey is going to require a tremendous investment of=
 resources over the long haul and we=E2=80=99re at the beginning of the beg=
inning. We=E2=80=99re at the first 1% of the first 1% of our market penetra=
tion.

I look at it in a bunch of different ways. I think every UI in the web and =
beyond the web, even native, will become a React-powered UI. That=E2=80=99s=
 just a fact. Even if it=E2=80=99s not specifically React, it=E2=80=99ll be=
 a framework like it. This transformation is unstoppable, the decoupling of=
 the monolith, unstoppable. The movement of on-prem workloads into the clou=
d and the edge, unstoppable. The rise of AI applications, unstoppable. So w=
e are on a very, very, very long journey to get to where we want to be.

I think having raised that funding at an attractive valuation, with frankly=
 not a ton of work on our side, just because of our incredible customer tes=
timonial =E2=80=94 a lot of venture capitalists would come to us with the h=
omework of they went and interviewed our customers and it was just amazing =
to hear. I was actually thankful to the VCs that they were like,=E2=80=9DWo=
w=E2=80=9D. It was a good moment for me to reflect on all this hard work is=
 paying off. Look at what these customers are saying, these firms would go =
out and say, =E2=80=9CWhat would happen if Vercel shuts down tomorrow=E2=80=
=9D? And what they were hearing back was, =E2=80=9CWell, my business ceases=
 to exist=E2=80=9D.

So I think the difference between Vercel, luckily, knock on wood, yada yada=
, is we run the mission-critical workload for companies. We either run the =
engineering workflow or we run the .com. So yes, 2023 looks different from =
2021 and there=E2=80=99s more pressure and CFOs are taking a second or thir=
d look at where money is going, but Vercel is very aligned with the heart o=
f your company and it=E2=80=99s not a thing that=E2=80=99s fleeting in resp=
onse to the ebbs and flows of the market sentiment. It=E2=80=99s a very end=
uring investment for us, for our customers and for our investors. So we rea=
lly haven=E2=80=99t seen any problems.

*Well that=E2=80=99s a pretty ambitious statement to close on, I love it! I=
 was going to ask you, =E2=80=9CWhat is it like being an infrastructure, De=
vOps provider when everyone just wants to talk about AI?=E2=80=9D, but I th=
ink you made the case, which is this is a real picks and shovels bit where =
it actually has nothing to do with the AI flow per se, but hey, you got to =
host it somewhere. You=E2=80=99ve got to actually interact with it somewher=
e and that=E2=80=99s where you see Vercel being the obvious choice.*

*GR:* Totally.

*Very good. Well Guillermo, thank you for coming on Stratechery. It=E2=80=
=99s quite interesting and we=E2=80=99ll look forward to seeing how things =
go.*

*GR:* Really enjoyed the interview. Thank you so much, Ben.
